questions.scenario: A checkout service handles 20,000 requests per minute. The release changes payment authorization and has no recent production equivalent. The team proposes starting at 25 percent for five minutes because staging passed. Which first step is safer?

options.text: Start at 10 percent for five minutes so the release gathers a large sample before the payment window changes.
Start at 25 percent but require payment success and latency to remain inside the staging thresholds.
Start with a much smaller slice, define a watch window, and increase only after release-split gates pass.
Start at 5 percent and promote on request count alone once the canary has processed 5,000 requests.

questions.explanation: High traffic gives a small canary enough samples quickly, while the payment change warrants a small initial blast radius and a defined observation window. Ten or 25 percent exposes more users than needed before production evidence exists. Request count is useful, but promotion still requires technical and business gates rather than volume alone. See [Argo Rollouts canary strategy](https://argo-rollouts.readthedocs.io/en/stable/features/canary/).

questions.scenario: The canary serves 5 percent of traffic. The global checkout 5xx rate rises only from 0.10 percent to 0.24 percent, but metrics have no version label. What evidence is missing for a defensible promotion decision?

options.text: Release-split errors, latency, and checkout success for canary and baseline.
A longer global observation window with the same aggregate service metrics.
Canary pod CPU and memory compared with the stable fleet over the same window.
Request totals for each version without outcome, latency, or business-result dimensions.

questions.explanation: The healthy baseline can dilute a failing 5 percent slice in an aggregate chart. Release-split errors, latency, and business outcomes support a like-for-like decision. A longer aggregate window preserves the dilution problem. Resource metrics and request totals add context but cannot show whether canary users successfully complete checkout. See [OpenTelemetry metric attributes](https://opentelemetry.io/docs/concepts/signals/metrics/).

questions.scenario: An internal admin API receives 12 requests per hour. A 1 percent canary has received no requests after 45 minutes, and all charts are flat. What should the team do before promotion?

options.text: Increase the canary to 10 percent immediately and use the next scheduled admin request as the validation sample.
Extend the current 1 percent watch window until one organic request reaches the canary.
Promote after readiness and a direct health check pass, then evaluate business behavior at full traffic.
Send a controlled cohort or synthetic traffic to the canary until the gate has enough evidence.

questions.explanation: No requests means no behavioral evidence. A controlled cohort or synthetic traffic creates repeatable evidence without depending on one random organic request. Increasing weight may also help, but changing exposure before defining the evidence and gate is less controlled. Readiness and health checks do not prove the admin transaction. See [Istio traffic shifting](https://istio.io/latest/docs/tasks/traffic-management/traffic-shifting/).

questions.scenario:
During the 10 percent step, the dashboard shows:

| Signal | Stable | Canary |
|---|---:|---:|
| 5xx | 0.08% | 2.9% |
| p95 | 180 ms | 430 ms |
| Checkout success | 99.4% | 95.1% |

What should the controller do?

options.text: Pause at 10 percent and extend the window to determine whether the canary converges toward the stable baseline.
Set canary traffic to zero and mark the release failed.
Return to 5 percent while investigating, because the service-wide error budget is not yet exhausted.
Hold at 10 percent and require one additional successful synthetic checkout before promotion.

questions.explanation: The canary is materially worse across independent technical and business signals, so the predefined failure action should remove its traffic. Extending or reducing the slice continues exposing users after a decisive gate failure. One synthetic success cannot outweigh sustained release-split regressions. See [Argo Rollouts analysis and progressive delivery](https://argo-rollouts.readthedocs.io/en/stable/features/analysis/).

questions.scenario: A user can add a discount through the canary and submit payment through stable. The versions interpret discount records differently. Which design best protects the experiment?

options.text: Use random request-level weighting and require both versions to read the new discount format during the rollout.
Route only discount creation to canary while payment requests remain on stable until promotion.
Use a stable user cohort or session routing and keep data formats compatible across both versions.
Use session affinity for traffic but allow canary-only writes because stable remains the read path.

questions.explanation: A stateful user journey needs consistent exposure, and both versions must understand shared data during the release. Random request weighting can split one journey across versions. Routing only one operation by version still crosses incompatible contracts, while affinity alone cannot protect stable from data it cannot read. See [Istio traffic shifting](https://istio.io/latest/docs/tasks/traffic-management/traffic-shifting/).

questions.scenario: The checkout team is defining an automated decision gate before the first production traffic shift. Which ingredients belong in that customer-facing canary gate?

options.text: A service-wide error-budget burn alert evaluated without release or version dimensions.
Canary versus baseline error and latency comparisons over a defined window.
A business signal such as payment authorization or checkout completion.
A readiness gate plus a minimum canary request count, without comparison to the stable version.
A predefined failure action that pauses or returns canary weight to zero.

questions.explanation: A useful gate compares release-specific technical signals, checks the outcome users care about, and defines what failure does. A service-wide burn alert remains useful as a safety net but can hide a small failing canary. Readiness and sample size prove availability and volume, not parity with stable behavior. See [Google SRE monitoring guidance](https://sre.google/sre-book/monitoring-distributed-systems/) and [Argo Rollouts canary strategy](https://argo-rollouts.readthedocs.io/en/stable/features/canary/).
